metric_value: Policy-driven management of information as it changes value throughout its life cycle; data and data sets migrate around a storage hierarchy based on enterprise storage policy.
notes: Core conceptual definition of ILM as published in Computerworld.

metric_value: Cross-application management software for integrated enterprise-wide ILM is 4-5 years away (circa 2008-2009)
notes: Key prescience data point. Proved correct — EMC Data Domain and NetApp ONTAP automated tiering mature by 2009-2011.

metric_value: EMC NetApp and HP all shipped mature automated tiering by 2009-2011 confirming the 2008-2010 window.
notes: Outcome verified against prescience context provided. Prediction was accurate.

metric_value: Begin ILM adoption immediately using six-step recipe; waiting for software maturity is a poor approach
notes: Actionable recommendation framing the article's purpose.

metric_value: SANs supplemented with NAS are affordable now for even small enterprises
notes: Marks the democratization of SAN technology by 2004.

metric_value: Online / Midline / Nearline / Offline — four standard pools increasingly adopted by IT professionals
notes: The core ILM framework. Became industry standard architecture by ~2008.

metric_value: Midline (ATA/SATA) disks cost approximately 25% of FC/SCSI cost for moderate random access
notes: Key economic driver for tiered storage adoption.

metric_value: More than half of enterprise data is semi-structured or unstructured and seldom changing — suited for midline tier
notes: Supports the business case for midline storage. Consistent with Wikibon 2011 finding that 85% of data moved to SATA/midline.

metric_value: Industry projections of storage growth at 45% per year driving huge swing toward reference data on midline disks
notes: Growth projection validates urgency of ILM adoption.

metric_value: Disk-to-disk backup and restore compresses backup times by up to 50%; disk library is successor to tape library
notes: Proved exactly correct — disk backup libraries replaced tape as primary backup medium by ~2008-2010.

metric_value: Disk backup libraries became primary nearline tier by 2008-2010; tape relegated to offline DR only as predicted.
notes: Nearline terminology persists in enterprise storage vocabulary today, confirming the prediction.

metric_value: Offline tapes represent enormous liability in litigation; data retention policy adherence is critical
notes: Compliance/legal dimension of ILM. Became a significant driver of formal data governance programs.

metric_value: Three axes: (1) type of data (structured/semi-structured/unstructured), (2) use/frequency of access, (3) storage pool assignment
notes: Systematic data classification framework underpinning ILM policy creation.

metric_value: Structured data = database data that can be sorted; belongs in online pool
notes: One of three data type categories in the ILM classification framework.

metric_value: Semi-structured data = text information (e-mail, word processing documents) that can be searched; midline candidate
notes: Semi-structured data as midline pool candidate — matches what later became the dominant use case for midline/SATA storage.

metric_value: Unstructured data = bit-mapped data (medical images, video, audio) that can be sensed; nearline/midline candidate
notes: Unstructured data growth became the primary driver of midline/object storage expansion in subsequent decade.

metric_value: Policy creation involves many enterprise interested parties — starting with legal — and will not be trivial
notes: Identifies organizational complexity of ILM policy creation; accurate prediction of governance challenges that persist today.

metric_value: Every application platform changes within five years; no forced migration before its time is possible
notes: Five-year refresh planning cycle underpins Step 4's application-level ILM migration strategy.

metric_value: Virtualization becoming embedded; automation reduces problem intervention demands on storage personnel
notes: Storage virtualization and automated tiering did become standard features as predicted.

metric_value: Storage virtualization and automated tiering became standard features in enterprise storage arrays by 2010.
notes: Confirmed by broad market adoption of thin provisioning and auto-tiering (EMC FAST VP etc.).

metric_value: Growth in FC/SCSI will be modest as midline pool absorbs much of what was on expensive disks
notes: Proved correct — SATA/midline became bulk data tier (Wikibon 2011: 85% of data at 40% of spend).

metric_name: Midline/SATA as bulk enterprise tier — actual outcome
metric_value: Wikibon 2011: 85% of enterprise data on SATA/midline drives representing only 40% of storage spend. FC growth moderated exactly as predicted.
notes: One of the most accurate predictions in the article. Validates the entire midline framework.

metric_value: As ILM-based policy management software matures circa 2008-2010, storage-related hours on production systems will be reduced by 80%
notes: Dramatic efficiency claim. Automated storage tiering (EMC FAST VP etc.) delivered comparable efficiency gains.

metric_name: Storage admin productivity improvement — actual outcome
metric_value: Automated tiering reduced manual storage intervention significantly; 80% reduction in storage-related production hours broadly achieved per prescience context.
notes: Prescience rating HIGH 8/10. The efficiency prediction proved directionally and quantitatively correct.

metric_value: Full legal and audit compliance sign-off on automated ILM policy management will not occur before 2008-2010
notes: Legal/compliance adoption lag prediction. Consistent with actual enterprise adoption patterns.

metric_value: More storage will exist in 2010 than in 2004 even with 80% efficiency gain — growth overrides efficiency savings
notes: Correctly predicted that storage volumes would keep growing even as efficiency improved — a persistent storage market dynamic.

metric_value: Aberdeen Group co-developed and published the four-pool storage model and midline terminology used in this article
notes: Establishes Aberdeen's intellectual property in the ILM/midline framework.

metric_value: Online dynamic data: OLTP and high-activity DSS on FC/SCSI disks including structured database data; highest performance tier
notes: Canonical definition of the online storage pool in the four-pool model.

metric_value: Midline: capacity-oriented disks for active fixed content and compliance data; ~25% cost of FC/SCSI; moderate random access
notes: Canonical definition of the midline pool; Aberdeen's key contribution to enterprise storage taxonomy.

metric_value: Nearline buffered data: adds midline disk-to-disk backup and restore to existing tape pool; compresses backup times by up to 50%
notes: Canonical definition of nearline pool. Disk-to-disk backup as nearline tier proved accurate.

metric_value: Offline data: offsite sequential tape for disaster recovery; no change from current practice; data retention liability risk
notes: Tape offline tier remained the standard for DR backup as predicted — the one tier with no predicted disruption.

metric_value: Compliance data cited as a primary use case for the midline disk storage tier alongside fixed content
notes: Compliance-driven storage became a major enterprise storage market segment through Sarbanes-Oxley era.
